translation.content: Can you reinvent the human voice? The oldest musical instrument in existence, our voices are the foundation of music as we know it, and few push its limits in electronic music today like Lyra Pramuk. On her masterful debut album, Fountain, the Berlin-based artist's voice was the only instrument. No drums, no synths—just her voice warped, synthesised and layered to achieve orchestral-like electronics. Five years on from her breakout, Pramuk's practice has steadily evolved, with a continued interest in ideas of folk and futurism, and how technology can enable us to build community beyond boundaries of time and space. There's a holistic nature to her artistry, one that extends seamlessly into her DJing. As she explains in the Q&A accompanying this week's RA Podcast, she sees her work as "part of a continuum of sacred, folkloric and communal music represented by many different subcultures and communities across the world." It's an approach that, like much of Pramuk's output, feels explicitly choral in nature. The first voice we hear on the mix comes from Lonnie Holley, his woozy Southern drawl calling out, "Earth will be there to catch us when we fall." (Although, you have to wonder, if we keep flogging the Earth, will it?) More follow across an eye-catching tracklist, from the poetry of friends (Nadia Marcus) to esoteric '80s French chantresses (Anne Gillis), while Pramuk wraps an extensive amount of her own forthcoming material around tracks from SD Laika, Leyland Kirby, rRoxymore and 33EMYBW, to name a few. By the end, voice and noise become indistinguishable—stammering, glitchy vocals colliding with an orchestra of instruments. The effect is mesmerising. Unfolding as one continuous composition, RA.979 exists somewhere between an ambient set at Kwia and the main stage at Unsound, voices and textures blurred into a fluid tapestry of sound. It's a singular offering to the series from a singular artist. —Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I've been very busy. I just announced my new label pop.soil and a new single and video. I have some exciting shows coming up. I've been really inspired lately. It's a nice time for me, as challenging and confusing as the world is. And I have some more big announcements coming soon ;) Your debut album, Fountain, was rich in narratives of futurism, technology and folk. Five years on, how do these elements continue to influence your work? These elements and themes are still very present in my creative process and in my daily life. I feel myself exploring different aspects of my background and different styles of folk, from both acoustic and electronic musical lineages, and what "folk" even means as a social and political marker in the 2020s. I got a lot more into my connection to nature and the universe in the last few years. Obviously the technological and political landscape is shifting very quickly now. It's scary but somehow also gives me energy. You've recently launched pop.soil to "facilitate meaningful discourse around art's evolving role in culture." Where do you see electronic music's role in this dynamic? The electronic music space is for me a continuum of possibility and emergent materiality. It's an important political space and also an important communal gathering space. Rave is still important—it animates us and draws us to each other. I think we will need rave a lot more in the coming years, as machine learning technologies and commercial AI tools take off, I feel that electronic music increasingly becomes a space where artists can reclaim our identities and agency as well... or pose new modes of working with identity in new ways, as Holly Herndon is doing. This era is obviously also ripe for new methods of exploitation and capture of humans and artists—that's inevitable, but I don't want to give too much energy to that right now. You recently collaborated with Sam Smith and Moses Sumney on a project celebrating Sylvester, a pioneering trans voice in music history. This project was really a dream. We were approached to work on a cover for Red Hot's TRANSA compilation. (Red Hot is the charity organisation that put out some of the first celebrity compilation albums to raise awareness for the AIDS crisis in the early '90s). Moses and I ultimately chose to honour Sylvester in our collaboration, and then we began this sketch and produced the first demo together. Sam joined us later in the process. Sylvester is one of the musical icons for me, and more people need to know and honour Sylvester. The whole TRANSA compilation is deeply touching, especially given the current manifestation of the country's ongoing war against trans people. How does honouring past voices shape your work, and are there any other artists you'd like to highlight? It's super important to me. I'm constantly referencing other artists I love in my creative process. I see my work as part of a continuum of sacred, folkloric and communal music represented by many different subcultures and communities across the world. The sacred aspect is something I'm owning more publicly now as well—in the majority of human history, music has been central to sacred community rituals. I feel different artists' influence in rotating cycles. Currently I'm especially thanking, honouring and feeling grateful for: Missy Elliott, Meredith Monk, Odetta, C.C. Hennix, Lonnie Holley, Anne Gillis and Carl Stone (to name a few). Outside of music and art, you've expressed a passion for astrology. How does this feed into your musical practice? I've always tapped into our cosmological clock. This influences my holistic experience of the world, but also inflects my sense of poetry, my sense of time, from the microscopic to the intergalactic scale, and even my belief in concepts of self-autonomy and determinism to a certain degree. Studying astrology and quantum physics have made me much more cognisant of my place in a system of things. Studying astrology has made my life and art more complex and vast and future-oriented. I feel my soul and my work expand beyond the limitations of neoliberal capture, even beyond the limitations of my own ego. I have a lot I can say about that, but I'll leave it there for now. What qualities do you look for in music when you're DJing? And can you tell us the idea behind the mix? I'm very interested in groove, texture, vocal and poetic quality, also well-recorded sounds and interesting or original productions or musical styles. I like to showcase diverse musical styles as a coherent whole, bringing various music cultures and lineages into a dramatic and compelling narrative. My approach to DJing is very cinematic and theatrical, yet also visceral, rooted in live music performance and my years as a raver. For this mix, I explored a kind of downtempo psychedelic trance style that sways throughout many times, genres and voices. The base idea was to select from a folder of many unfinished sketches of mine and mix those with other tracks. About 30 percent of what you hear are unreleased sketches of mine. This took me a long time to make. I'm a producer before I'm a DJ, so I did it in Ableton with all the clips in Re-Pitch mode (vinyl-style clips) with nothing beatmatched and I did it all by ear, warping clips faster or slower to match with the previous track. I'm a pretty good editor, so I wanted to explore this concept at a slower pace in a DAW, as a producer, and be really precise about it. But I've been live mixing some blends similar to this already. I was thinking: what if a high-energy set was built not by BPM, but by poetic intention? I really believe that music has the power to be cathartic and healing and deeply spiritual. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Two I think about a lot that affect all of us are light health and plastics pollution. We need natural sunlight on our skin and in our eyes, and we need darkness at night—this cycle is extremely important for our health and well-being, the artificial blue light is making us really sick. Plastics pollution is equally a huge problem. These are issues I hope more people will understand and fight for in the coming years. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? That's maybe very cliché, but I would have to say Berghain. I "grew up" there, partying a lot between 2014 to 2017. That's where I found my voice—I discovered I was trans there. My experiences really formed me, shaped me, in so many ways, into the woman, person and artist I am today.
translation.blurb: Mesmerising dance floor tapestries from a singular experimental musician.
